channel.title: OT/ICS Threat Intel - Water
channel.description: Curated OT/ICS advisories, incidents and threat intelligence. Editorial by Mike Holcomb in partnership with UtilSec.

item.title: [Critical] Remote-access appliances under active attack: SonicWall SMA 1000 zero-days exploited, federal patch deadline 17 July
item.description: SonicWall has confirmed active exploitation of two Secure Mobile Access 1000 flaws after investigating multiple cases. CVE-2026-15409 is a CVSS 10.0 unauthenticated server-side request forgery in the Work Place interface; CVE-2026-15410 (CVSS 7.2) lets an authenticated admin run OS commands via the Appliance Management Console. CISA added both to the KEV catalog on 14 July, with federal agencies required to fix by 17 July. Water and wastewater utilities are disproportionately exposed here for structural reasons: remote access is not a convenience but the operating model. Lean teams cover distributed treatment plants and lift stations after hours, and integrators dial in for dosing, pump and SCADA support. When the appliance carrying that traffic is the thing being exploited, the compromise arrives wearing a valid session. - SonicWall PSIRT (SNWLID-2026-0008) / CISA KEV / The Hacker News - SonicWall advisory & KEV addition 14 Jul 2026 — reporting 15 Jul; exploitation ongoing

item.title: [High] Cal Water finds no evidence hackers touched OT after Iranian group Handala claimed it could disrupt the water supply
item.description: California Water Service, working with Mandiant, found no evidence of activity against its operational technology following a cyberattack claimed by the Iran-linked group Handala. The distinction matters. Handala published roughly 5 GB of data including customer personal information and credentials for the RTKBase platform, and asserted it could have disrupted the water supply but chose not to. Cal Water’s investigation indicates no operational disruption to water or wastewater systems and no OT intrusion. This is a useful case study in how OT incident reporting goes wrong: the claim travelled faster and further than the finding. Treat the data exposure as real and the OT-disruption claim as unsubstantiated — while noting that leaked platform credentials are exactly the sort of thing that makes a future intrusion cheaper. - SecurityWeek / Cal Water / Mandiant - Claim 12 Jun 2026; Cal Water findings reported 25 Jun 2026

item.title: [High] Lantronix serial-to-IP converter flaw exploited in the wild — the forgotten boxes bridging pump and dosing gear
item.description: Attackers are exploiting CVE-2025-67038 in Lantronix serial-to-IP converters, one of the flaws disclosed in April through the BRIDGE:BREAK research project. For water utilities this is a particularly awkward class of device. Serial-to-IP converters are how decades-old pump controllers, chlorine analysers, level sensors and remote lift-station gear got onto Ethernet in the first place — usually installed by an integrator during a retrofit, never added to an asset register, and never patched since. Compromising the bridge gives access to whatever sits behind it, and the device typically lives inside the network you consider protected. Exploitation is confirmed and follows an explicit OT threat warning. For most utilities the honest first step is discovery, not patching: you cannot fix converters you do not know you own. - CVE-2025-67038 - Disclosed Apr 2026 — exploitation reported 25 Jun 2026; ongoing

item.title: [High] CISA flags OpenPLC v3 for water and wastewater, alongside two Schneider energy advisories
item.description: CISA’s 9 July batch of three ICS advisories includes OpenPLC v3, explicitly flagged as used in Water and Wastewater Systems as well as Energy. The other two cover Schneider Electric Easergy MiCOM Px40 protection relays and PowerChute Serial Shutdown. OpenPLC deserves a moment of honesty from the sector: it is open-source soft-PLC software that turns up in small-utility deployments, pilot projects, training rigs and integrator proof-of-concepts — and, not infrequently, in things that quietly became production. Because it has no vendor account team to send you a notice, advisories against it tend to go unread by the people running it. Check the advisory for affected versions and confirm whether OpenPLC exists anywhere in your estate, including on hardware someone stood up temporarily. - CISA ICS advisories / WaterISAC - CISA advisories published 9 Jul 2026

item.title: [Critical] July ICS Patch Tuesday: critical DoS flaws in Rockwell Logix controllers, plus a Schneider IGSS SCADA code-execution fix
item.description: The July 2026 cycle carries two items with direct water-sector weight. Rockwell Automation published 12 advisories including three critical denial-of-service flaws in CompactLogix, ControlLogix, Compact GuardLogix and GuardLogix controllers that can force a major non-recoverable fault — in plain terms, a controller that stops and does not come back on its own. Allen-Bradley Logix hardware runs a large share of treatment-plant and pumping control, so that is a process-availability issue, not a data one. Schneider Electric separately fixed a high-severity flaw in IGSS, its SCADA product, where crafted files can execute arbitrary code. Siemens published nine advisories, six critical, including a CVSS 10 authentication bypass in Opencenter X and fixes for Simatic S7-1500. VDE CERT added five more covering Codesys and Wago among others. - Siemens ProductCERT / Schneider / Rockwell PSIRT / SecurityWeek - Published 14 Jul 2026 (Patch Tuesday) — reporting 15 Jul

item.title: [High] Labcenter Proteus 9 and Digi serial servers among CISA’s 7 July batch touching water systems
item.description: CISA released seven ICS advisories on 7 July. Labcenter Proteus 9 is flagged for Water and Wastewater Systems as well as Energy, and Digi International PortServer TS and Digi One SP IA9 — serial device servers of the same family as the Lantronix converters currently being exploited — also feature. The rest of the batch covers Hitachi Energy PROMOD V and e-mesh EMS, Siemens Mendix Studio Pro and SINEC OS, and the Hydro-Québec Le Circuit Électrique charging-station backend. The Digi entries are worth pairing with the Lantronix item in Incidents: two advisories in the same fortnight against the humble serial-server category suggest researchers are looking hard at it, which historically precedes attackers doing the same. - CISA ICS advisories / WaterISAC - CISA advisories published 7 Jul 2026

item.title: [High] BeyondTrust patches critical auth-bypass flaws in the privileged remote access tooling integrators use
item.description: BeyondTrust disclosed two authentication-bypass vulnerabilities on 7 July in Remote Support (RS) and Privileged Remote Access (PRA). CVE-2026-40138 (CVSS 9.2) comes from improper validation of authentication data and could let a network-positioned attacker bypass access controls to reach the appliance, including elevated accounts; CVE-2026-40139 involves improper processing of RS authentication requests. Exploitation of both depends on a specific authentication configuration being enabled, which narrows the exposed population. Neither is under active exploitation and no public proof-of-concept exists. Cloud customers were patched on 21 April; self-hosted RS/PRA at 25.3.2 or earlier needs attention. Read this alongside the SonicWall top story: the common thread is that third-party remote access into plant networks is where the sector’s risk actually concentrates. - CVE-2026-40138 - Disclosed 7 Jul 2026

item.title: [Info] NIST publishes an OT backup quick-start guide — aimed squarely at utilities with no spare capacity
item.description: NIST released SP 1339, an OT Backup Quick Start Guide intended to improve industrial cyber resilience and speed incident recovery. The short-format framing is deliberate, and it suits the water sector better than most guidance does: the constraint at a small or mid-size utility is rarely awareness, it is that one or two people already carry compliance, engineering and operations. What the guide gets right is treating backup as a restoration problem. In a treatment plant the artefacts that determine recovery time are PLC logic, HMI and SCADA configurations, historian baselines, dosing setpoints and the engineering-workstation image needed to push any of it back — not the file server. Those are the things most often missing, stale, or sitting on the same domain as the systems they are meant to restore. - NIST SP 1339 - Published 19 Jun 2026
